title: WhatsApp / Chat — System Design
description: Design a real-time messaging system: deliver 1:1 and group messages instantly to online users, buffer them for offline users, and sync history across a user's devices — without ever losing an accepted message. The recurring themes are persistent connections, a message-delivery state machine, idempotent multi-device delivery, presence, and group fanout.

nodes.label: Mobile / Web Client
nodes.description: User's app; holds a persistent connection and a local message store.

nodes.label: Connection Load Balancer
nodes.description: Routes new connections to gateway nodes; sticky per connection.

nodes.label: WebSocket Gateway
nodes.description: Holds millions of long-lived connections; one user's devices map here while online.

nodes.label: Session / Presence Registry
nodes.description: Maps userId/deviceId -> which gateway holds the live connection; tracks online status.

nodes.label: Chat Service
nodes.description: Validates, persists, and routes messages; owns delivery semantics.

nodes.label: Per-User Inbox Queue
nodes.description: Materialized per-recipient inbox of references (server_msg_id) into the durable log — not a second copy of the body. Holds queued/delivered state; entries are deleted or marked delivered on device ack. Derived from MsgDB, so on any inconsistency the log wins and the client dedups by (conversation_id, seq).

nodes.label: Message Store
nodes.description: Durable messages, sharded by conversation/user for history sync.

nodes.label: Group Fanout Service
nodes.description: Expands a group message to each member's queue/gateway.

nodes.label: Conversation Membership Store
nodes.description: Conversation -> versioned member list (the conversations / conversation_members tables), sharded by conversation id. Unifies 1:1 and group membership; fanout reads a pinned membership_version.

nodes.label: Push Notification Service
nodes.description: APNs/FCM bridge to wake offline devices.

nodes.label: Media Service + Blob Store
nodes.description: Stores client-encrypted image/video/voice attachments; messages carry a media id resolved to a signed CDN URL.

nodes.label: Observability + Control Plane
nodes.description: Metrics, tracing, and SLOs (send-to-ack, send-to-delivered, queue depth, fanout lag, registry staleness, reconnect rate, duplicate rate, per-gateway saturation). Feeds admission control / backpressure that throttles sends, fanout, and reconnect drain under overload.

functional: Send a 1:1 message; deliver in real time if the recipient is online.
Buffer messages for offline recipients and deliver on reconnect.
Group messaging: a message goes to all current group members.
Sync message history across a user's multiple devices.
Abuse controls: blocked users, group-send authorization, and send rate limits.
nonFunctional: Real-time delivery: online-to-online latency well under a second.
Reliable: an accepted message is never lost, even across crashes.
Ordered: messages within a conversation arrive in send order.
Scale to hundreds of millions of concurrent persistent connections.
Highly available; tolerate gateway and region failures.
Privacy: the server stores ciphertext payloads and routes on metadata only; plaintext is never persisted server-side (E2E key exchange scoped as a follow-up).

Multi-region: clients home to a nearby region; single-region-per-conversation with cross-region log replication for DR (not active-active per message). Target near-zero RPO for acked messages (synchronous quorum within region + async cross-region) and an RTO of minutes via conversation-ownership failover.
Retention: messages and media have a configurable TTL; account deletion and group-membership history are honored.

capacity.label: Concurrent connections
capacity.note: ~300M devices each hold one persistent socket at peak.

capacity.label: Memory per connection
capacity.note: TLS state + kernel socket buffers + app metadata (userId, deviceId, cursor). At 30 KB, 1M sockets ≈ 30 GB RAM — so a gateway is RAM- and FD-bound, not CPU-bound.

capacity.label: Connections per gateway
capacity.note: Bounded by memory (~30 GB), file descriptors, and heartbeat CPU. 300M / 1M ⇒ ~300 gateways, plus headroom and per-region spread.

capacity.label: Gateway fleet
capacity.note: ~300 for steady state + ~50% headroom to absorb a region/AZ loss and reconnect storms without exceeding per-node limits.

capacity.label: Heartbeat observations
capacity.note: 300M sockets pinging every ~50s ⇒ 6M inbound heartbeats/s, ~15K/s per gateway. These hit the gateway locally — they are NOT 6M central registry writes (see registry load).

capacity.label: Fanout amplification
capacity.note: Avg group ~8 members, p95 ~50, max 1024. A group send writes one log row but N delivery/inbox events. Blended delivery ≈ 5–8M delivery events/s from 1M sends/s.

capacity.label: Receipt / control multiplier
capacity.note: Each delivered message generates delivered + read receipts, plus typing/presence frames. Control frames dominate the per-socket event rate.

capacity.label: Registry reads (lookups)
capacity.note: One lookup per delivery TARGET, not per send — so reads track delivery events after fanout (5–8M/s), not the 1M sends/s. Cut further by caching a user's device→gateway set at the ChatSvc for the conversation's hot window, and by co-locating the lookup with fanout (one read per recipient user, fan to its devices).

capacity.label: Registry writes (leases)
capacity.note: Decouple central writes from the 6M/s heartbeat observations: gateways absorb every heartbeat LOCALLY and refresh the central lease lazily (e.g. once per ~30–60s per device, or only on state change). So central writes ≈ connect/disconnect churn + lazy lease refresh ≈ 0.5–1M/s, not 6M/s. Lease TTL (~90s) self-expires stale entries.

capacity.label: Registry partitioning
capacity.note: Key by (user_id) so a user's devices co-locate; shard by user_id hash across the regional registry that owns that user. Hot users/groups are spread by device, and a degraded shard fails open to the offline path (enqueue + push) rather than dropping sends. No single node sees the full write rate.

capacity.label: Avg message size
capacity.note: Media goes via blob store, not the message path; only a ~50-byte mediaId reference rides the message.

capacity.label: Offline queue storage
capacity.note: Assume p50 offline gap hours, p95 days. ~hundreds of M users × tens of buffered messages × ~200 B inbox entry ⇒ tens of TB, capped by TTL (e.g. 30 days) and compaction.

capacity.label: Media path
capacity.note: ~100K media msgs/s × ~MB blobs ⇒ ~100s of GB/s of blob bandwidth served by object storage + CDN, fully off the text message path.

capacity.label: Reconnect storm
capacity.note: One gateway loss = 1M clients reconnect. With jittered backoff over ~5 min ⇒ ~3K reconnects/s, ~3K registry writes/s + queue drains — absorbable. Without jitter, a 1M/s thundering herd would overwhelm the registry and LB.

api.description: Open a persistent WebSocket. The gateway authenticates, registers the device in the session registry with a TTL lease, and drains anything missed. The client sends its per-conversation cursors (or a resume token) so the server replays only what's new.
api.request: Upgrade: websocket  { authToken, deviceId, protocolVersion, resumeToken?, cursors: { c_7: 410, c_12: 88 } }
api.response: { sessionLease: 90, serverTime, missed: [...messages after each cursor...] }

api.description: Client sends a message frame over its socket. Server persists, assigns a per-conversation seq, routes, and acks. Idempotent on (conversationId, senderId, senderDeviceId, clientMsgId).

Group send uses the SAME send_message frame as 1:1 — the only difference is that conversationId names a group conversation. The server checks the sender is a current member allowed to post (authorization), enforces the per-(user, conversation) send rate limit, resolves group membership at the current membership version, and fans out per recipient — skipping members in user_blocks against the sender. (REST POST /v1/conversations/{id}/messages exists only for server-to-server or non-socket clients; interactive clients always send over the socket so receipts and ordering share one path.)

Receipts. ack_delivered is emitted by a recipient device when a message lands on it; ack_read when the user views it. The server records per-device delivery and rolls it up to a user-level delivered/read state, then routes the receipt back to the sender (and the user's other devices) as a control frame.
api.response: { "ok": true }   // sender later receives: { receipt: "read", conversationId, upToSeq, byUser }

api.description: History sync / catch-up. A device passes its last-applied per-conversation cursor and receives ordered messages after it from the durable log. Used on reconnect, on a new device, and to reconcile after a missed push. Idempotent: the client applies by (conversationId, seq) and ignores anything it already has.

messages.label: apply by (conv, seq); page if hasMore

api.description: Media negotiation, off the message path. The client requests a signed upload URL, PUTs the (client-encrypted) blob to object storage, then sends a normal message carrying the returned mediaId. Recipients resolve mediaId to a signed download URL served via CDN.
api.request: POST { contentType: "image/jpeg", size: 248000, sha256 }  ⇒  { mediaId, uploadUrl, expiresAt }
api.response: GET /v1/media/m_med_9  ⇒  302 signed CDN URL (authorized to conversation members only)

api.description: Control frames, rate-limited. typing is a throttled, fire-and-forget hint (debounced ~5s, never persisted, not fanned out to large groups). presence_subscribe registers interest in a contact's online/last-seen, which is derived from the session registry's heartbeat TTL and respects the contact's last-seen privacy setting.
api.response: no ack for typing; presence pushed as { userId, status: "online"|"last_seen", at }

api.description: Fast reconnect after a socket drop. The client presents its resumeToken and cursors; if the session lease hasn't expired the server re-binds the connection and replays only the gap, avoiding a full re-auth and full history scan during reconnect storms.
api.response: { "resumed": true, "missed": [ ... ] }   // or { resumed:false } ⇒ client falls back to /connect

api.description: Abuse + retention controls. Block/unblock writes user_blocks (checked on fanout). 'Delete for everyone' tombstones a message (keeps the (conv, seq) row for ordering, purges body + media). Account deletion schedules a lifecycle job that tombstones the user's messages, expires media objects, drops live sessions, and retains only the membership-version audit history required for group integrity.
api.response: { ok: true }  // deletions are async lifecycle jobs; tombstones replicate to devices via sync

dataModel.name: conversations
dataModel.note: One row per 1:1 or group conversation; gives conversation_id a stable owner, type, and current membership version. A 1:1 is just a 2-member conversation, so 1:1 and group share one delivery path.

dataModel.name: conversation_members
dataModel.note: Membership with versioning, so 'all current members' is a deterministic snapshot at send time. Replaces the old group_members.

dataModel.name: messages
dataModel.note: Durable message log = source of truth, sharded by conversation_id, ordered by (conversation_id, seq). server_msg_id is a globally unique snowflake; seq is the per-conversation order — two distinct keys, not one overloaded id.

fields.name: client_msg_id
fields.type: uuid — UNIQUE (conversation_id, sender_id, sender_device_id, client_msg_id) for dedup

fields.name: body
fields.type: encrypted blob (ciphertext; server never sees plaintext)

fields.name: sender_key_id
fields.type: string (which sender/device key encrypted body; server routes on this, can't decrypt)

fields.name: deleted_at
fields.type: timestamp | null (tombstone: 'delete for everyone' / retention; row kept, body purged)

dataModel.name: inbox_entries
dataModel.note: Per-recipient materialized delivery row (the MsgQ inbox) — an optimization for wake/drain, NOT the only source of per-device delivery. It is per-USER: any one of the user's devices draining and acking clears the entry; other devices catch up from the durable log via their own per-device cursor (so a stale device isn't blocked on the inbox). Keyed so redelivery is idempotent.

fields.name: server_msg_id
fields.type: bigint (reference into messages; body is NOT copied)

dataModel.name: delivery_state
dataModel.note: Per-device delivery + read state that rolls up to a user-level aggregate. Keyed to support multi-device receipts (one device may have read while another hasn't).

dataModel.name: conversation_cursors
dataModel.note: Per-device sync cursor per conversation: the highest seq the device has applied. Drives history sync and resume — a returning device asks for everything after its cursor. A separate user-level read cursor backs read receipts.

dataModel.name: devices
dataModel.note: Two halves shown as one compact entity. DURABLE identity (device_id, user_id, platform, device_key_id) lives in the primary store and survives reconnects. EPHEMERAL session state (gateway_id, region, lease_expires_at, last_heartbeat) lives ONLY in the hot TTL registry (Redis), keyed by user_id; it self-expires on missed heartbeats and is rebuilt when gateways re-register — never the source of truth for delivery.

fields.name: device_key_id
fields.type: string (durable public-key identity for E2E; published to peers, never the private key)

fields.name: gateway_id
fields.type: string — EPHEMERAL (registry only): which gateway holds the live socket

fields.name: lease_expires_at
fields.type: timestamp — EPHEMERAL: heartbeat TTL; past it ⇒ offline

dataModel.name: user_blocks
dataModel.note: Who has blocked whom. Read on the send/fanout path to drop messages and presence/typing from a blocked sender; the sender is not told (silent drop).

dataModel.name: rate_limits
dataModel.note: Quota counters enforced at the gateway/ChatSvc edge for send spam and typing/presence floods. A fast counter store (Redis) with sliding windows; exceeding a bucket rejects or sheds the frame before it reaches fanout.

fields.name: subject_key
fields.type: string PK — e.g. user:u_42:send, device:d_3:typing, conv:g_55:send

dataModel.name: device_keys (E2E key directory)
dataModel.note: Public identity + prekeys per device, fetched by peers to establish E2E sessions. The server stores and serves public material only — it never holds private keys or plaintext, which is exactly what 'routes on metadata, can't read bodies' means. Full key-exchange protocol (e.g. X3DH/Double Ratchet) is the scoped follow-up; this entity is the minimum to make the ciphertext claim credible.

dataModel.name: media_objects
dataModel.note: Metadata for blobs in object storage; the message carries only media_id. Lifecycle/TTL govern retention; content is client-encrypted.

fields.name: enc_metadata
fields.type: bytes (client key id / nonce; server stores ciphertext only)

patterns.name: Persistent connection (WebSocket)
patterns.what: Keep a long-lived socket per device so the server can push messages instantly.
patterns.whenToUse: Real-time delivery where polling latency/overhead is unacceptable.

patterns.name: Connection routing registry
patterns.what: A fast lookup of device -> gateway so any service can reach an online user's socket.
patterns.whenToUse: When millions of stateful connections are spread across many gateway nodes.
patterns.group: Route a Message to the Recipient's Connection

patterns.name: Offline message queue
patterns.what: Persist undelivered messages per recipient and drain on reconnect.
patterns.whenToUse: Recipients are frequently offline but must not miss messages.

patterns.name: Idempotent message id
patterns.what: Client-generated id dedups retries so a flaky network doesn't double-send.
patterns.whenToUse: At-least-once delivery over unreliable mobile networks.

patterns.name: Fanout-on-write (groups)
patterns.what: Expand a group message to each member at send time.
patterns.whenToUse: Group sizes are bounded; per-member delivery state is needed.

patterns.name: Per-conversation ordering
patterns.what: Assign a monotonic sequence per conversation so messages display in order.
patterns.whenToUse: Chat where out-of-order display is confusing.

patterns.name: Per-device sync cursor
patterns.what: Each device records the highest per-conversation seq it has applied and asks only for newer messages on reconnect.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-device sync where push, queue drain, and history sync all read one durable log and must not double-deliver.

patterns.name: Membership-version snapshot
patterns.what: Pin a group send to the membership version at send time so the recipient set is deterministic.
patterns.whenToUse: Group fanout where membership can change concurrently with sends.

patterns.name: Session lease (heartbeat TTL)
patterns.what: Hold the device->gateway mapping under a TTL refreshed by heartbeats, so dead sessions self-expire.
patterns.whenToUse: Large stateful connection fleets where gateways can crash and presence must stay accurate.
patterns.group: Route a Message to the Recipient's Connection

patterns.name: Jittered reconnect / admission control
patterns.what: Spread mass reconnects with randomized backoff and admit connections gradually to avoid thundering herds.
patterns.whenToUse: When a gateway or region loss drops millions of sockets at once.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Poll the Server for New Messages
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. The client sends a message with an ordinary HTTP POST; the server writes it to a messages table. To receive, each client polls the server every few seconds (GET /messages?since=...) over plain HTTP and the server returns any new rows.

This is correct and trivial to build — and it exposes the two problems the rest of the design exists to solve. First, latency: a message sits unseen until the recipient's next poll, so 'real-time' is really 'every few seconds', and shortening the interval only makes it worse. Second, load: do the arithmetic — 300M clients polling every 3 seconds is 100M polls/sec hitting the database before anyone has actually sent a message, and the overwhelming majority return zero new rows. That is a crushing, wasteful read load whether or not anyone is talking.

So the baseline motivates everything that follows: replace polling with a persistent connection the server can push down, so delivery is instant and the server only does work when there's an actual message.

view.caption: The client POSTs over HTTP through the load balancer to the Chat Service, which persists each message to the Message Store; recipients repeatedly GET-poll the same path for new rows.

steps.decisionPrompt: If every client polls the server every 3 seconds for new messages, what does that cost at hundreds of millions of users — and how 'real-time' is it?
whyNow: Establishing the polling baseline first makes it obvious why a persistent connection is needed — it removes both the latency and the wasted-poll load in one move.

recap.after: Messages POSTed and stored; recipients poll the DB for new rows.
recap.newRisk: Polling is high-latency and floods the database with mostly-empty reads; the server can't push, so delivery is never truly real-time.

traps.trap: Shipping HTTP polling as 'good enough' for chat.
traps.why: Short poll intervals multiply database load across hundreds of millions of clients while still delivering messages seconds late.
traps.instead: Hold a persistent connection per device so the server pushes messages the instant they arrive.

steps.title: 2. Persistent Connections via WebSocket Gateways
description: Polling can't deliver in real time at this scale. Each device opens a long-lived WebSocket to a gateway node that holds the connection open and can push messages down instantly.
A gateway holds ~1M connections; a connection load balancer spreads new connections across the fleet. The connection is stateful, so it's sticky to one gateway for its lifetime.
Gateways are dumb pipes: they own the socket and (de)serialize frames, but delivery logic lives in the chat service behind them.

Because the sockets are long-lived, rolling deploys can't just drop a gateway: drain it first — stop accepting new connections, signal its clients to reconnect elsewhere with jittered backoff, let them migrate, then retire the node. Graceful draining turns a deploy into the same brief, bounded reconnect as a crash (covered in step 7) rather than a mass disconnect.

view.caption: The client connects over WSS to the Connection Load Balancer, which assigns it to a WebSocket Gateway that forwards sent messages to the Chat Service.

options.name: Persistent WebSocket (default)
options.description: Each device holds one long-lived full-duplex WebSocket to a sticky gateway node that keeps the socket open and pushes frames down the instant a message arrives.
pros: A single full-duplex connection lets the server push a message the instant it arrives — true real-time.
No repeated request overhead per poll, so it scales to hundreds of millions of idle connections cheaply.
Bidirectional: typing indicators, receipts, and presence flow both ways over one socket.
cons: Each gateway must hold millions of long-lived connections, with state to recover on node loss.
Mobile networks drop sockets, so clients need reconnect-and-resync logic.
Stateful connections complicate load balancing and rolling deploys.

view.caption: The client holds one long-lived WebSocket through the Connection Load Balancer to a sticky WebSocket Gateway, which relays messages to the Chat Service.

options.name: HTTP long-polling
options.description: The client issues an HTTP request the server holds open until a message arrives or it times out, then immediately re-polls.
pros: Works everywhere HTTP does — no special protocol, friendly to proxies and old clients.
Server holds the request open and replies when a message arrives, so it's near-real-time.
Simpler to reason about than persistent sockets; degrades to normal request/response.
cons: Each delivered message costs a full request/response cycle plus a re-poll, multiplying overhead.
Server-to-client push for the next message waits for the client to re-establish the poll.
Connection churn is high at scale, wasting capacity versus one stable socket.

view.caption: The Connection Load Balancer holds the client's HTTP request open at a Long-Poll Gateway until a message arrives, which then relays to the Chat Service.

options.name: Periodic polling
options.description: Clients ask the server 'anything new?' on a fixed timer, with no held connection or server push.
pros: Dead simple: clients ask 'anything new?' on a timer — trivial server logic.
Stateless on the server, so any node can answer any poll.
Easy to cache and rate-limit.
cons: Latency equals the poll interval, so 'real-time' is really 'every few seconds'.
Hundreds of millions of clients polling every few seconds is enormous wasted load when nothing changed.
No server push at all — strictly worse for a chat product.

view.caption: The client polls the Chat Service poll endpoint via the load balancer every 3 seconds, and the service persists messages to the Message Store.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why a persistent WebSocket rather than HTTP long-polling or periodic polling?

concepts.definition: A node that terminates many long-lived bidirectional sockets and relays frames.
concepts.whyItMatters: Enables server push with low latency and no polling overhead.
concepts.example: One gateway holds 1M sockets; the cluster holds hundreds of millions.

whyNow: The connection model is the foundation — everything about routing, presence, and delivery depends on where the live socket lives.

recap.before: Polling: messages POSTed and stored, recipients poll for new rows.
recap.after: Devices hold live sockets to gateways; server can push.
recap.newRisk: Connections are spread across many gateways — how does a sender's message find the recipient's gateway?

traps.trap: Putting delivery logic in the gateway.
traps.why: Stateful gateways become hard to scale and deploy; a restart drops connections AND logic.
traps.instead: Keep gateways thin; route to a stateless chat service for delivery.

steps.title: 3. Route a Message to the Recipient's Connection
description: When a message arrives, the chat service looks up the recipient in the session registry: which gateway(s) currently hold their devices' sockets. Crucially it resolves all active devices for the user — and routes to the sender's own other devices too, so a message sent from the phone appears on the laptop (multi-device sync starts here, not as an afterthought).

Registry entries are lease-based: each holds gateway id, device id, region, and protocol version under a TTL refreshed by heartbeats, so a crashed gateway's sessions self-expire instead of lingering. If the chat service pushes to a gateway and the socket is already gone (a stale entry), that push misses; the service marks the session stale and falls back to the offline path (enqueue + push wake) rather than dropping the message.

Keep the registry in a fast store (e.g. Redis) keyed by user id (a user's devices co-locate), sharded by user-id hash within the user's home region; it's rebuildable from gateways re-registering, so it isn't the durable source of truth for messages. Mind the workload units: it's one lookup per delivery target (so reads track delivery events after fanout, ~5–8M/s, not the ~1M sends/s), while writes are kept off the heartbeat path — gateways absorb heartbeats locally and refresh the central lease lazily, so central writes are connect/disconnect churn, not 6M/s. A degraded shard fails open to the offline path rather than dropping sends.

view.caption: The WebSocket Gateway hands a sent message to the Chat Service, which persists it to the Message Store, checks the Session / Presence Registry, and pushes to the recipient's online gateway.

options.name: Session registry lookup (default)
options.description: On a message, the chat service looks up the recipient's device-to-gateway mapping in a fast session registry and tells exactly those gateways to push the frame.
pros: A presence registry maps user/device to its current gateway, so the chat service routes only to the right gateways.
No wasted fanout — a message goes precisely to the devices that are online.
Handles three devices on three gateways by looking up all active sessions for the user.
cons: The registry is on the hot path and must be a fast, highly-available cache.
Registry staleness (just-disconnected device) causes a delivery miss that falls back to the offline path.
Every connect/disconnect writes to the registry, adding churn.

view.caption: The Chat Service persists to the Message Store, looks up the recipient's gateway in the Session / Presence Registry, and pushes the frame to exactly that WebSocket Gateway.

options.name: Pub/sub broadcast per user
options.description: Each user has a broker topic; gateways subscribe for their connected users and the chat service just publishes, letting the broker fan out to subscribed gateways.
pros: Each user has a topic; gateways subscribe for their connected users and the chat service just publishes.
No central registry lookup — the broker handles fanout to whichever gateways are subscribed.
Naturally delivers to multiple devices: all subscribed gateways receive the publish.
cons: Hundreds of millions of per-user topics strain the broker's subscription state.
Without per-user routing, a publish may touch gateways that no longer hold the user.
Broker becomes a critical, high-fanout dependency to scale and operate.

view.caption: The Chat Service publishes to the recipient's per-user topic on the Pub/Sub Broker, which delivers to whichever WebSocket Gateways subscribe for that user.

steps.decisionPrompt: A user has three devices on three different gateways. How does a message reach all of them?
whyNow: Routing across a sharded connection fleet is the core real-time problem; the session registry is how it's solved.

concepts.definition: A live mapping of device/user -> the gateway holding its connection.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets any backend node locate and push to an online device.
concepts.example: Lookup u_42's devices -> [gw7, gw19] -> push to both.
concepts.group: Route a Message to the Recipient's Connection

concepts.definition: A registry entry that expires unless refreshed by the socket's periodic heartbeat.
concepts.whyItMatters: A crashed gateway's sessions vanish automatically — no separate cleanup, and routing never targets a dead socket for long.
concepts.example: 90s lease; missed heartbeats past TTL ⇒ entry expires ⇒ user shown offline.
concepts.group: Route a Message to the Recipient's Connection

recap.before: Sockets exist but are unreachable from the backend.
recap.after: Any message can find an online recipient's (and sender's other) devices.
recap.newRisk: Recipient may be offline — the message still must not be lost.

traps.trap: Treating the user as single-device and routing to one socket.
traps.why: Real users have a phone, a laptop, and the web — a message must reach all their devices, including the sender's own others.
traps.instead: Resolve all active device sessions for the user and push to each gateway.

traps.trap: Trusting a registry hit and dropping the message if the push misses.
traps.why: Sessions go stale between disconnect and lease expiry, so a 'present' entry can point at a dead socket.
traps.instead: On a missed push, mark the session stale and fall back to enqueue + push wake.

steps.title: 4. Reliable, Ordered, Idempotent Delivery
description: Persist the message durably before acking the sender — that's what makes 'never lost' true across crashes. The conversation's shard owns a monotonic seq counter and assigns the next value on persist, so all sends in a conversation are totally ordered by the single partition that owns it (concurrent sends serialize on that shard; no cross-shard ordering is promised or needed). server_msg_id is a separate globally-unique snowflake for addressing.

Be precise about the guarantee: transport is at-least-once, and correctness comes from making both ends idempotent. The send is deduped on the full scope (conversationId, senderId, senderDeviceId, clientMsgId) — clientMsgId alone is not enough, since the same value from another device or conversation is a different message. Recipient delivery is idempotent too: a device applies by (conversationId, seq) and silently drops anything at or below its cursor, so a redelivery from the queue, a history sync, and a live push can all carry the same message without showing it twice. Say 'at-least-once + idempotent apply', not 'effectively-once'.

Delivery state is tracked per recipient device and rolled up to a user-level aggregate: a message can be delivered to the phone but not the laptop, and read on one device. Receipts (delivered, read) flow back as small control frames through the gateway and advance the relevant cursor.

view.caption: The WebSocket Gateway sends each message to the Chat Service, which persists it to the Message Store and pushes it to the recipient's online device for ordered, idempotent delivery.

steps.decisionPrompt: The client sends, the network drops the ack, the client retries. How do you avoid a duplicate message?

concepts.definition: A client-generated id the server uses to dedup retried sends.
concepts.whyItMatters: Mobile networks make retries common; without dedup, users see double messages.
concepts.example: Second send with the same clientMsgId returns the original serverMsgId.

concepts.definition: A monotonic counter per conversation defining message order.
concepts.whyItMatters: Guarantees consistent ordering across devices regardless of arrival timing.
concepts.example: Devices sort by sequence, not by local receive time.

whyNow: Reliability, ordering, and dedup are the correctness heart of chat; getting them explicit is the senior signal.

recap.after: Durable, ordered, dedup'd messages with per-device receipt state.
recap.newRisk: An offline recipient needs a buffer; receipts and presence add control traffic.

failureDrills.scenario: Chat service crashes after persisting but before acking.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Client retries with the same clientMsgId; the server matches the existing row and re-acks the original serverMsgId without duplicating.
failureDrills.mitigation: Persist-before-ack + idempotent dedup on the full (conversationId, senderId, senderDeviceId, clientMsgId) tuple.

failureDrills.scenario: The owning conversation shard is unavailable during sequence allocation.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The send is not acked (no false 'sent'); the client retries with the same clientMsgId, and once the shard recovers — or its replica is promoted — the seq is allocated exactly once thanks to the dedup key. No gap or duplicate in the conversation order.
failureDrills.mitigation: Single shard owns the conversation's seq counter (serialize on it); replicate the shard, fail writes closed rather than allocating a second authority, and dedup on retry.

flows.name: Persist-before-ack with idempotent dedup
flows.note: The message is durably stored and ordered before the sender is acked; a retry with the same clientMsgId returns the original id instead of duplicating.

flows.name: Delivered + read receipts (per device, rolled up)
flows.note: The recipient device emits the ack through its gateway; the chat service records per-device state, rolls it up to a user-level aggregate, and pushes the receipt back through the sender's gateway to the sender's device.

messages.label: set device state; roll up user delivered/read

traps.why: The same clientMsgId from another device or another conversation is a different message; scoping only by clientMsgId can collapse distinct messages or fail to catch real retries.
traps.instead: Make the dedup key the full (conversationId, senderId, senderDeviceId, clientMsgId) tuple.

traps.trap: Showing a message twice across live push, queue drain, and history sync.
traps.why: All three paths read the same durable log, so a message can legitimately arrive more than once.
traps.instead: Make recipient apply idempotent: order by (conversationId, seq) and drop anything at or below the device cursor.

steps.title: 5. Offline Delivery and History Sync
description: Pick the buffering granularity deliberately: the inbox is per-user, not per-device. One inbox_entry per (recipient_user_id, conversation_id, seq) references the durable log; whichever device reconnects drains it, and each device tracks its own applied cursor so all of a user's devices converge on the same ordered view without N copies of every message. (Per-device queues would multiply storage by the device count for no correctness gain, since the log already orders everything.)

If the recipient has no live session, the entry sits in the queue and we wake the device with a push notification (APNs/FCM) so it reconnects and drains. Push is only a hint — the durable queue/log is the delivery guarantee; a dropped push just delays, it never loses.

Reconnect, queue drain, and history sync all read the same durable log, so the client must apply idempotently: it sends its per-conversation cursor on connect and drops anything at or below it, so a message arriving via push, via drain, and via a catch-up sync is shown exactly once. A returning device (or a brand-new one) is just a drain starting from an older cursor. Bound the queue: entries carry a TTL (e.g. 30 days) and compact, so a user offline for weeks doesn't accumulate unbounded state — they fall back to a history-sync read from the log.

view.caption: The Chat Service checks the Session / Presence Registry, enqueues offline messages to the Per-User Message Queue (drained on reconnect), and wakes the device via the Push Notification Service.

options.name: Per-user message queue (push to inbox) (default)
options.description: An offline recipient's messages accumulate in their durable per-user queue, which the gateway drains down the new socket on reconnect.
pros: Each user has a durable queue; messages for an offline user accumulate and drain on reconnect.
Delivery is push-on-reconnect, so the client gets everything it missed in order.
Decouples senders from recipient availability — the sender's write always succeeds.
cons: Storing per-user queues for hundreds of millions of users is significant state.
A user offline for days on a new device needs a separate history-sync path beyond the queue.
Queue growth for very inactive users must be bounded or archived.

view.caption: For an offline recipient the Chat Service enqueues to the durable Per-User Message Queue (drained on reconnect) and wakes the device via the Push Notification Service.

options.name: Pull from message store on reconnect
options.description: Messages are persisted once in the message store; on reconnect the client pulls everything after its last-seen id rather than draining a dedicated queue.
pros: Messages are persisted once in the message store; on reconnect the client pulls everything after its last-seen id.
No per-user queue to maintain — the durable store is the single source of truth.
History sync to a new device is the same query with an earlier cursor.
cons: Reconnect triggers a potentially large catch-up read, spiking load after outages.
The client must track and send a reliable last-seen cursor per chat.
Waking an offline device still needs a separate push to trigger the pull.

view.caption: The Push Notification Service wakes the device; on reconnect the client pulls messages after its cursor as the Chat Service reads the Message Store from the last-seen id.

steps.decisionPrompt: Recipient is offline for two days, then reconnects on a new phone. What should they see?
whyNow: Offline is the common case on mobile; the durable per-user queue + push is the standard answer and ties back to 'never lost'.

flows.name: Offline enqueue, push wake, idempotent drain
flows.note: Offline recipient gets an inbox entry + a wake push; on reconnect the client presents its cursor and the gateway drains only what's after it, so nothing is shown twice.

concepts.definition: The highest per-conversation seq a device has applied; sent on reconnect to fetch only newer messages.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets push, queue drain, and history sync share the durable log without double-delivery, and makes a new device just a drain from seq 0.
concepts.example: Laptop at c_7:410 reconnects ⇒ receives 411,412 only.

concepts.definition: A per-recipient reference to a logged message, holding queued/delivered state — not a copy of the body.
concepts.whyItMatters: Decouples senders from recipient availability while keeping one canonical message; entries are deleted on ack and TTL-bounded.

concepts.definition: A delete marker that keeps the (conversation, seq) row for ordering but purges the body/media, replicated to devices via sync.
concepts.whyItMatters: Implements 'delete for everyone', retention TTL, and account deletion without leaving seq gaps that would break ordering and cursors.
concepts.example: Delete-for-everyone sets deleted_at; devices syncing past that seq render 'message deleted'.

traps.trap: Treating a push notification as the delivery channel.
traps.why: APNs/FCM are best-effort and can be dropped or throttled; relying on them loses messages.
traps.instead: Deliver from the durable queue/log; use push only to wake the device to drain.

traps.trap: Letting offline queues grow without bound.
traps.why: A user offline for weeks (or a dead device) accumulates unbounded state across hundreds of millions of users.
traps.instead: TTL + compact inbox entries; fall back to a history-sync read from the log for long gaps.

recap.after: Offline users buffered + woken; history syncs across devices.
recap.newRisk: Group messages multiply this work per member — need fanout.

steps.title: 6. Group Messaging and Fanout
description: A group message must reach every member as of a specific membership version. The fanout service stamps the send with the group's current membership_version, reads exactly that snapshot, then for each member does the same online/offline decision as 1:1: push to their gateway or write an inbox entry. Snapshotting matters — if someone is added or removed mid-send, the version pins a deterministic recipient set, so 'all current members' has one answer.

Store one canonical message in the log and fan out lightweight inbox-entry references plus per-member delivery rows — never copy the body into N queues. That keeps groups storage-cheap and makes per-recipient receipts (delivered/read counts) fall out of the same delivery_state rows as 1:1.

Authorization is part of fanout, not an afterthought: check the sender is a current member allowed to post (from conversation_members), enforce the per-(user, conversation) send quota from rate_limits, and skip any member with a user_blocks row against the sender (a silent drop — the sender isn't told). A partial fanout failure is retried per member (the inbox-entry write is idempotent on (recipient, conv, seq)), not re-fanned to everyone.

Bound group size so fanout-on-write stays cheap. Beyond a threshold (broadcast channels, 10K+ members) switch that conversation to fanout-on-read: append once to a shared group log that members pull at their own cursor, trading instant push for bounded write cost.

view.caption: The Chat Service hands group messages to the Group Fanout Service, which lists members at the pinned membership version from the Conversation Membership Store and writes a lightweight reference into each Per-User Inbox Queue drained on reconnect.

options.name: Fanout-on-write to per-member queues (default)
options.description: On send, the fanout service writes the message into each current member's queue, so online members get it immediately and offline members find it waiting.
pros: On send, the fanout service writes the message into each member's queue, so delivery to online members is immediate.
Read path is trivial — each member just drains their own queue.
Offline members already have the message waiting when they reconnect.
cons: A 256-member group means 256 inbox-entry writes per message — write amplification (the body is stored once; only references fan out).
Very large groups make each send expensive and bursty.
Needs a membership-version snapshot so adds/removes mid-send have a deterministic recipient set.

view.caption: On send, the Group Fanout Service lists members from the Conversation Membership Store and writes the message into each member's Per-User Message Queue.

options.name: Fanout-on-read (shared group log)
options.description: The message is appended once to a shared group log that each member reads from on demand via their own per-group read cursor.
pros: The message is written once to a shared group log; members read from it on demand.
No write amplification — one append regardless of group size, ideal for huge groups.
Storage is a single copy per message, not one per member.
cons: Each member must track a per-group read cursor and pull, adding read-path complexity.
Online members don't get an instant push unless you still notify them to read.
A burst of members reading a hot group log creates read fanout instead.

view.caption: The Chat Service lists members from the Conversation Membership Store and appends the message once to the Shared Group Log, which each member's WebSocket Gateway reads at its own cursor.

steps.decisionPrompt: A 256-member group gets a message; 100 members are offline. What work happens?
whyNow: Group fanout reuses the 1:1 delivery primitives and is the natural scaling step; bounding group size is the tradeoff to name.

concepts.definition: Pinning a group send to the membership_version current at send time, so the recipient set is deterministic.
concepts.whyItMatters: Concurrent adds/removes can't make 'all current members' ambiguous or cause partial/duplicate fanout.
concepts.example: Send stamped v14 ⇒ fan out to members at v14 even if v15 lands mid-fanout.

concepts.definition: Per-user block edges (user_blocks) and per-(user, conversation) send quotas checked on the send/fanout path.
concepts.whyItMatters: Stops spam and harassment without leaking the block to the sender; keeps abuse work off the hot delivery loop.
concepts.example: Fanout skips a member who blocked the sender; a 200th send/min returns 429.

recap.after: Group messages fan out to a membership snapshot with per-member receipts.
recap.newRisk: Large groups and presence updates create fanout amplification; gateways are a scaling pressure point.

traps.trap: Fanning out to a live membership read instead of a pinned version.
traps.why: A concurrent add/remove during fanout yields a nondeterministic recipient set — some members miss the message, others get one they shouldn't.
traps.instead: Stamp the send with membership_version and fan out to that snapshot.

traps.trap: Copying the message body into every member's queue.
traps.why: A 256-member group would store the body 256 times; a hot broadcast group explodes storage and write cost.
traps.instead: Store one canonical message in the log; fan out lightweight inbox-entry references.

bottlenecks.issue: Huge group floods the fanout service.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Cap group size; for broadcast-scale, switch to pull/feed model.

bottlenecks.issue: Presence/typing updates amplify across large groups.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Throttle and batch presence; don't fan out typing to huge groups.

flows.name: Group message fanout to members
flows.note: One canonical message is fanned out to each member; per member the online/offline decision is made independently.

steps.title: 7. Presence, Media, and Gateway Resilience
description: Three remaining concerns, each with its own mechanism. Presence & typing: presence (online / last-seen) is derived for free from the session lease + heartbeats — no separate write path. Fan presence changes only to a user's contacts who are subscribed, debounce them (don't emit on every flap), respect the user's last-seen privacy setting, and never fan typing indicators to large groups. Typing is a throttled, never-persisted hint.

Media plane: media rides a separate path. The client requests a signed upload URL, PUTs the client-encrypted blob to object storage, then sends a normal message carrying only the mediaId; recipients resolve it to a signed CDN URL authorized to conversation members. The text message path never carries bytes.

Gateway & regional resilience: when a gateway dies its ~1M sockets drop and the session leases expire. Clients detect the drop and reconnect with jittered exponential backoff so 1M reconnects spread over minutes (~3K/s) instead of a thundering herd. On reconnect they present a resume token + cursors — the server replays only the gap rather than a full re-auth and history scan. The load balancer and gateways apply admission control, and queue drain is throttled, so the registry and fanout stay below their write budgets. A gateway loss is therefore a brief, bounded reconnect — not data loss and not a cascading overload.

view.caption: The WebSocket Gateway registers connections in the Session / Presence Registry, while clients upload and download blobs to the Media Service + Blob Store and the Chat Service validates media ids against it.

steps.decisionPrompt: A whole gateway node crashes with 1M connections. What is the user-visible impact?
whyNow: Closing on presence/media/resilience maps the remaining requirements and shows the design degrades gracefully under failure.

concepts.definition: Liveness inferred from periodic pings on the live socket plus a TTL in the registry.
concepts.whyItMatters: Accurate online/last-seen without a separate presence write path.
concepts.example: Missed heartbeats past TTL -> mark offline -> notify contacts.

concepts.definition: The mass simultaneous reconnect after a gateway/region loss, smoothed by randomized exponential backoff plus admission control.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without jitter, 1M clients reconnect at once and overwhelm the LB, registry, and queue drains — turning one node loss into a cascade.
concepts.example: 1M drops reconnect over ~5 min (~3K/s) instead of 1M/s.

concepts.definition: The control plane throttles sends, fanout, and reconnect admission when a metric crosses a threshold, instead of letting overload cascade.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bounds the blast radius of hot groups, queue lag, and reconnect storms so the system sheds load gracefully rather than collapsing.
concepts.example: Registry write lag high ⇒ LB admits reconnects slower; fanout lag high ⇒ ChatSvc slows group sends.

concepts.definition: Exactly one region owns a conversation's seq counter; on region loss, ownership fails over to the replica-holding region.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps ordering single-writer (no dual allocation/split-brain) while still surviving a region outage with bounded RPO/RTO.
concepts.example: Region A owns g_55; A fails ⇒ promote B's replica to own the counter, clients re-home.

recap.after: Presence, media, and graceful gateway failover handled.
recap.newRisk: Reconnect storms after a gateway loss; presence fanout cost — both bounded by batching and backoff.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Those clients detect the drop and reconnect (with jittered backoff) to other gateways, resume from their cursor, and drain queues. Reconnects spread over minutes, not a single spike.
failureDrills.mitigation: Stateless durable backend + rebuildable session registry + client jittered backoff + resume token + admission control on the LB/gateways.

failureDrills.scenario: Message persisted, queued, delivered to a device, but the delivered-ack is lost.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The inbox entry stays in queued state and is redelivered; the device, already past that seq, drops the duplicate and re-sends ack_delivered, which finally clears the entry.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent apply by (conversationId, seq) + idempotent inbox-entry key, so redelivery is safe.

failureDrills.scenario: A whole region's gateways fail (reconnect storm).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Clients re-home to a nearby region (DNS/anycast) and reconnect with jittered backoff + admission control, so the surviving regions absorb the load over minutes rather than a single spike.
failureDrills.mitigation: Cross-region capacity headroom + regional homing + jittered re-home backoff + admission control.

failureDrills.scenario: The primary region that OWNS a conversation is unavailable during active sends.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Sends acked before the outage are durable (in-region quorum) and survive; in-flight sends not yet acked are NOT shown as 'sent', so clients retry. Conversation ownership fails over to the region holding the replicated log: writes freeze briefly (RTO ~minutes) while a replica is promoted to own the seq counter, then resume. Because retries carry the same (conv, sender, device, clientMsgId), promotion can't create a duplicate or a seq gap. Async-replication lag means a tiny window of acked-but-not-yet-replicated messages (RPO target near-zero, bounded by replication lag); those replay once the primary returns.
failureDrills.mitigation: Single owner per conversation for the seq counter + synchronous in-region quorum for the ack + async cross-region replication + ownership failover (promote replica, freeze-then-resume) + idempotent retry. Clients discover the new home via DNS/control-plane redirect and only send to the current owner, avoiding split-brain dual-allocation.

flows.name: Media upload, send, download
flows.note: Bytes never touch the message path: the sender uploads to the blob store and sends a mediaId over its socket; the recipient device (not the gateway) resolves a signed CDN URL.

messages.label: resolve signed CDN URL + download blob

flows.name: Gateway crash, reconnect, resume, drain
flows.note: A gateway loss is a brief reconnect: the lease expires, the client backs off with jitter, resumes from its cursor, and drains only the gap.

messages.label: reconnect after jittered backoff (resume token + cursor)

traps.trap: Fanning presence/typing to everyone or on every change.
traps.why: Presence and typing for hundreds of millions of users amplify into a fanout flood that dwarfs message traffic.
traps.instead: Fan only to subscribed contacts, debounce, respect privacy settings, and never fan typing to large groups.

traps.trap: Letting clients reconnect immediately on gateway loss.
traps.why: A synchronized 1M-client reconnect is a thundering herd that takes down the LB, registry, and queue drains.
traps.instead: Jittered exponential backoff + admission control + resume-from-cursor so reconnects spread over minutes.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Real-Time Chat
finalDesign.description: Devices hold WebSocket connections to thin gateways; a lease-based session registry maps device -> gateway and feeds presence from heartbeats. The chat service persists each message to the durable log (per-conversation seq for order, dedup on (conversation, sender, device, clientMsgId)) before acking, then routes to all of the recipient's AND the sender's other devices: push to the gateway if online, else write a per-user inbox entry (a reference into the log — the log is the source of truth, the inbox is derived and reconciled by cursor) and send a wake push. Devices apply idempotently by (conversation, seq), so live push, queue drain, and history sync never double-show. Groups fan out per member to a pinned membership version; per-device delivery state rolls up to user-level delivered/read receipts; media rides a separate client-encrypted blob/CDN plane (only a mediaId on the message). Gateways are replaceable — clients reconnect with jittered backoff, resume from cursor, and drain queues; an observability/control plane drives backpressure (send, fanout, and reconnect-drain throttles). The durable log replicates cross-region for DR (single-region-per-conversation, not active-active): a message is acked only after in-region quorum (near-zero RPO) and one region owns each conversation's seq counter; on region loss, ownership fails over to the replica-holding region (freeze-then-resume, RTO ~minutes) and clients re-home via DNS/control-plane redirect, so idempotent retries can't dual-allocate or split-brain. E2E encryption is a layered extension: payloads are already ciphertext, so server-side routing, fanout, and receipts run on metadata only; key distribution is the follow-up.

view.caption: Clients connect over WSS through the Connection Load Balancer to WebSocket Gateways tracked in the lease-based Session / Presence Registry; the Chat Service persists to the durable Message Store, pushes to online devices (recipient and sender's others) or writes a Per-User Inbox Queue reference, fans out groups via the Group Fanout Service to a membership snapshot, wakes offline devices through push, and validates media against the blob store. An Observability + Control Plane feeds backpressure into the Chat Service (send/fanout throttles), the Gateways (shed/slow sends), and the Load Balancer (reconnect admission control).

functional.how: Persistent socket + session-registry routing + gateway push.
Per-user durable queue drained on reconnect; push to wake.
Fanout service expands to members and reuses delivery primitives.
Per-recipient delivery state; presence from heartbeats.
Durable log read by per-device conversation cursor; new/returning device is a drain from an older cursor.
Routing resolves all of a user's device sessions and the sender's other devices; per-device delivery state rolls up to user level.
Separate signed-upload / CDN-download blob plane; message carries only a mediaId.
Fanout checks sender authorization and skips blockers; send rate limits at the gateway.

nonFunctional.how: Direct push over live sockets, no polling.
Persist before ack; durable offline queue.
Sharded gateway fleet + rebuildable session registry.
Thin stateless gateways; lease-based registry self-heals; clients reconnect with jittered backoff, resume from cursor, and drain. Admission control prevents reconnect-storm cascades.
Server stores encrypted bodies and routes on metadata only; media is client-encrypted. E2E key exchange is a layered follow-up.
Cross-region durable-log replication for DR; clients re-home and resume from cursor. Single-region-per-conversation, not active-active.
TTL/compaction on inbox entries and media objects; account deletion and membership-version history honored.

tradeoff: Self-hosted gateways give the most control over per-connection memory, heartbeat tuning, and draining at 1M-socket scale, but you own scaling, rolling deploys, and connection migration.
Managed WebSocket services remove the connection-fleet ops but cap per-connection control and can be costly at hundreds of millions of idle sockets.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: An in-memory store with native TTL fits the hot, rebuildable lease table; a managed key-value DB with TTL trades a little latency for durability and zero ops.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A fast lease store with heartbeat TTL removes any need for a separate presence write path or a cleanup job for dead sessions.

tradeoff: Wide-column stores (Cassandra/Scylla) excel at the high-write, partition-by-conversation, append-ordered access pattern chat needs, but you operate compaction and hot-partition mitigation.
A managed globally-distributed DB (Spanner/Cosmos) simplifies cross-region replication and ordering at higher cost.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed auto-sharding store removes the need to hand-shard conversations and manage rebalancing.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: A durable log/broker (Kafka/Pulsar) gives replayable, partitioned fanout with backpressure; a managed queue (SQS/Pub/Sub) removes broker ops but offers weaker ordering/replay guarantees.
Talking to APNs/FCM directly is simple and cheap; a managed push hub abstracts per-platform tokens and retries at the cost of another dependency.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Push is only a wake hint — the durable queue is the delivery guarantee, so push provider choice never affects correctness.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Object storage + CDN is the standard for the media plane; self-hosting (MinIO/Ceph) is only worth it for data-residency or cost control at very large scale.

Self-hosted Prometheus/OTel gives full control of high-cardinality chat metrics (per-gateway saturation, fanout lag) but you operate the metrics store at scale; managed monitoring is turnkey but cardinality/retention can get expensive.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A solid metrics + control loop is what lets backpressure thresholds (registry write lag, queue drain rate) drive admission control automatically instead of manual intervention.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: A Redis-backed counter at the gateway gives precise per-(user, conversation) quotas with low latency on the send hot path; edge/WAF throttling catches volumetric abuse earlier but is coarser-grained for per-conversation spam.

E2E content keys live on devices (libsignal-style); the server-side KMS/HSM only protects server secrets (TLS, at-rest encryption, the public-key directory), never message plaintext. That split is what keeps 'server routes on metadata, can't read bodies' true.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Client-side E2E means no server-side message-decryption key exists to manage or leak — the server's KMS scope shrinks to transport/at-rest only.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Single-writer-region with async cross-region replication (Cassandra multi-DC, Aurora Global) keeps per-conversation ordering simple and gives near-zero RPO with minutes RTO; a synchronously-replicated global DB (Spanner) can offer stronger guarantees at higher write latency — usually overkill when conversations are region-homed.

say: Confirm real-time 1:1 + group, offline delivery, receipts, and history sync.
Pin sub-second delivery and 'never lost' as the hard guarantees.
Establish the scale: hundreds of millions of live connections.
Session registry to route to a recipient's gateway.
Delivery: per-conversation seq from the owning shard, dedup on (conv, sender, device, clientMsgId), per-device receipts rolled up to user level.
Offline: per-user inbox of log references + push wake; idempotent apply by (conv, seq) across push/drain/sync; per-device cursors for multi-device.
Groups: fanout to a pinned membership version, authorization + blocked-user checks; fanout-on-read for broadcast-scale.
Resilience: lease-based registry, jittered reconnect + resume, presence/media on separate paths.
Map requirements to mechanisms; call out the log-vs-inbox consistency boundary.
Tradeoffs: at-least-once + idempotent apply (not 'exactly-once'), fanout-on-write vs -on-read, presence/typing fanout cost.
Privacy: payloads are ciphertext so routing/receipts run on metadata; E2E key exchange and active-active multi-region are the senior extensions.

expectations: Uses WebSockets and a session lookup.
May miss dedup/ordering and offline handling.
Designs persist-before-ack, idempotent send (full dedup scope), and per-conversation ordering.
Handles the offline inbox + push wake and idempotent apply by (conv, seq).
Designs group fanout with a membership-version snapshot and per-device receipts.
Reasons about gateway sharding, reconnect storms, and a rebuildable lease-based registry — and gets the hot-path units right (reads per delivery target, writes off the heartbeat path).
Handles multi-device sync end to end (sender's other devices, per-device cursors).
Discusses presence fanout cost, conversation-ownership region failover (RPO/RTO), and E2E encryption tradeoffs.

followUps: How would you add end-to-end encryption without breaking server-side fanout/receipts?
How do you handle a reconnect storm when a whole region's gateways fail over?
How would you support very large broadcast groups (10K+ members)?
How do you keep presence accurate without flooding contacts with updates?
How would you order messages across multiple senders in a group fairly?

links.groupDescription: Real messaging systems that expose connection routing, fanout, message storage, reliability, and migration tradeoffs.
links.title: Real-time Messaging
links.why: Concrete production architecture for channel servers, gateway servers, presence servers, consistent hashing, and realtime fanout.

links.groupDescription: Real messaging systems that expose connection routing, fanout, message storage, reliability, and migration tradeoffs.
links.title: Migrating Millions of Concurrent Websockets to Envoy
links.why: Useful for the gateway tier: long-lived socket load balancing, regional ingress, hot restarts, and operational migration risk.

links.groupDescription: Real messaging systems that expose connection routing, fanout, message storage, reliability, and migration tradeoffs.
links.title: Migrating Messenger Storage to Optimize Performance
links.why: Shows a production message-history system with queued subscriber writes, storage migration, validation, buffering, and read-latency goals.

links.groupDescription: Real messaging systems that expose connection routing, fanout, message storage, reliability, and migration tradeoffs.
links.title: Under the Hood: Improving Messages
links.why: Grounds the baseline problems: disconnects, incorrect counts, missed messages, duplicates, and reliability-focused client architecture.

links.groupDescription: Real messaging systems that expose connection routing, fanout, message storage, reliability, and migration tradeoffs.
links.title: Building End-to-End Security for Messenger
links.why: Production discussion of default E2EE, multi-device constraints, encrypted message history, key rotation, and web-client tradeoffs.

links.groupDescription: Real messaging systems that expose connection routing, fanout, message storage, reliability, and migration tradeoffs.
links.title: How Discord Stores Trillions of Messages
links.why: Production message-store migration from Cassandra to ScyllaDB with hot partitions, latency, and operational-toil lessons.

links.groupDescription: Standards and protocol extensions for persistent connections, acknowledgements, receipts, read markers, multi-device sync, archives, and group chat.
links.title: The WebSocket Protocol
links.why: Core protocol reference for the persistent bidirectional browser/mobile connection used by online chat delivery.

links.groupDescription: Standards and protocol extensions for persistent connections, acknowledgements, receipts, read markers, multi-device sync, archives, and group chat.
links.title: MQTT Specifications
links.why: Useful comparison point for mobile messaging transports, QoS levels, topic fanout, and low-bandwidth persistent connections.

links.groupDescription: Standards and protocol extensions for persistent connections, acknowledgements, receipts, read markers, multi-device sync, archives, and group chat.
links.title: RFC 6121: XMPP Instant Messaging and Presence
links.why: Canonical standard for instant messaging and presence routing semantics, including one-to-one chat and delivery rules.

links.groupDescription: Standards and protocol extensions for persistent connections, acknowledgements, receipts, read markers, multi-device sync, archives, and group chat.
links.title: XEP-0198: Stream Management
links.why: Defines stanza acknowledgements and stream resumption, directly mapping to reconnect, retry, and duplicate-handling concerns.

links.groupDescription: Standards and protocol extensions for persistent connections, acknowledgements, receipts, read markers, multi-device sync, archives, and group chat.
links.title: XEP-0184: Message Delivery Receipts
links.why: Precise protocol model for sender-visible delivered receipts without confusing delivery with human read acknowledgement.

links.groupDescription: Standards and protocol extensions for persistent connections, acknowledgements, receipts, read markers, multi-device sync, archives, and group chat.
links.title: XEP-0333: Displayed Markers
links.why: Useful for read receipts and group read state because it marks progress up to a message rather than acking every message.

links.groupDescription: Standards and protocol extensions for persistent connections, acknowledgements, receipts, read markers, multi-device sync, archives, and group chat.
links.title: XEP-0280: Message Carbons
links.why: A standard design for synchronizing both sides of a conversation across a user's multiple online devices.

links.groupDescription: Standards and protocol extensions for persistent connections, acknowledgements, receipts, read markers, multi-device sync, archives, and group chat.
links.title: XEP-0313: Message Archive Management
links.why: Reference model for server-side archives, history queries, pagination, and multi-device catch-up from durable history.

links.groupDescription: Standards and protocol extensions for persistent connections, acknowledgements, receipts, read markers, multi-device sync, archives, and group chat.
links.title: XEP-0045: Multi-User Chat
links.why: Standard group-chat room model for membership, roles, invitations, moderation, and message reflection to occupants.

links.groupDescription: References for durable logs, high-write message stores, pub/sub, mobile push, private media delivery, and operational monitoring.
links.title: Cassandra: A Decentralized Structured Storage System
links.why: Foundational high-write distributed storage paper for partitioned, replicated message history and inbox-style workloads.

links.groupDescription: References for durable logs, high-write message stores, pub/sub, mobile push, private media delivery, and operational monitoring.
links.title: Apache Kafka Design
links.why: Explains durable logs, partitions, replication, consumer groups, and replay patterns useful for delivery events and fanout workers.

links.groupDescription: References for durable logs, high-write message stores, pub/sub, mobile push, private media delivery, and operational monitoring.
links.title: Redis Pub/Sub
links.why: Good contrast for presence and live fanout: fast pub/sub with at-most-once semantics, not a durable message-delivery path.

links.groupDescription: References for durable logs, high-write message stores, pub/sub, mobile push, private media delivery, and operational monitoring.
links.title: Firebase Cloud Messaging
links.why: Official reference for waking mobile clients, targeting devices, and using push as a notification-to-sync mechanism.

links.groupDescription: References for durable logs, high-write message stores, pub/sub, mobile push, private media delivery, and operational monitoring.
links.title: About FCM Messages
links.why: Useful for TTL, priority, collapsible versus non-collapsible behavior, and why chat alerts need different treatment from sync hints.

links.groupDescription: References for durable logs, high-write message stores, pub/sub, mobile push, private media delivery, and operational monitoring.
links.title: Sending Notification Requests to APNs
links.why: Official APNs provider reference for iOS push delivery, notification priority, and device-token addressing.

links.groupDescription: References for durable logs, high-write message stores, pub/sub, mobile push, private media delivery, and operational monitoring.
links.title: Download and Upload Objects with Presigned URLs
links.why: Reference design for direct client media uploads/downloads without proxying large blobs through the chat service.

links.groupDescription: References for durable logs, high-write message stores, pub/sub, mobile push, private media delivery, and operational monitoring.
links.title: Use Signed URLs
links.why: Shows time-bound private CDN delivery for media attachments and why blob access should be authorized separately from chat events.

links.groupDescription: References for durable logs, high-write message stores, pub/sub, mobile push, private media delivery, and operational monitoring.
links.title: Monitoring Distributed Systems
links.why: Grounds chat operations in user-visible latency, errors, saturation, reconnect storms, delivery lag, and alert quality.

links.groupDescription: Secure messaging protocols and abuse-control references for E2E, multi-device, group messaging, and encrypted-platform moderation.
links.title: The X3DH Key Agreement Protocol
links.why: Core asynchronous E2E key agreement model for sending an initial encrypted message to an offline recipient.

links.groupDescription: Secure messaging protocols and abuse-control references for E2E, multi-device, group messaging, and encrypted-platform moderation.
links.title: The Double Ratchet Algorithm
links.why: Explains per-message key evolution, forward security, break-in recovery, and out-of-order encrypted-message handling.

links.groupDescription: Secure messaging protocols and abuse-control references for E2E, multi-device, group messaging, and encrypted-platform moderation.
links.title: The Sesame Algorithm: Session Management for Asynchronous Message Encryption
links.why: Directly addresses multi-device encrypted messaging, device add/remove, per-device mailboxes, session state, retries, and receipts.

links.groupDescription: Secure messaging protocols and abuse-control references for E2E, multi-device, group messaging, and encrypted-platform moderation.
links.title: Private Group Messaging
links.why: Clear explanation of encrypted group fanout tradeoffs, including pairwise sends and shared ciphertext/key optimization for media.

links.groupDescription: Secure messaging protocols and abuse-control references for E2E, multi-device, group messaging, and encrypted-platform moderation.
links.title: Technology Preview: Signal Private Group System
links.why: Advanced reference for private group membership, server-side authorization without plaintext membership, and duplicate-entry controls.

links.groupDescription: Secure messaging protocols and abuse-control references for E2E, multi-device, group messaging, and encrypted-platform moderation.
links.title: Jettisoning Junk Messaging in the Era of End-to-End Encryption: A Case Study of WhatsApp
links.why: WhatsApp-specific abuse-control research showing what can be studied or mitigated when message contents are end-to-end encrypted.
